mainText:
**The ‘Honest Power’ of American Trains**
By Peter C. Meilaender

On May 12, 2026, The Morning Dispatch posed the question, "Why Can’t America Build Trains?" However, on June 11, I witnessed a spectacular reminder that we once could. That day, Union Pacific’s Big Boy No. 4014 continued a cross-country journey that began on April 10 near Sacramento, moving through western New York.

The world’s largest operating steam locomotive started its day near Buffalo, traveled to Silver Springs, briefly paused at Letchworth State Park, and then made a whistle stop in Hornell at 2 p.m. Hornell is significant as it is where Amtrak’s high-speed Acela trains are manufactured, making it the largest passenger train manufacturing site in the country.

In Hornell, I, along with my family and thousands of spectators, eagerly awaited Big Boy’s arrival. With temperatures soaring to 90 degrees and no shade in sight, we secured our spots hours in advance. The atmosphere was vibrant, with a food truck thriving, local businesses providing bottled water, and a live band playing classic railroad tunes.

As the anticipated whistle of Big Boy drew near, the crowd grew animated, smartphones raised high for photos. Suddenly, he came into view: clouds of steam billowed, followed by the massive black locomotive rolling down the tracks. It stopped just 20 feet in front of us, and when the steam whistle blew, it was startlingly loud, a sound that resonated deep within the heart of the crowd.

Big Boy, measuring 133 feet long and over 16 feet tall, is half the length of a Boeing 747 and weighs 600 tons. His wheels towered over the mechanics attending to the train. The engineer, atop the cab, waved to the crowd, embodying the power and presence of this engineering marvel.

Standing before Big Boy evokes a sense of immense power. Speed, strength, noise, steam, and smoke combine to create a magnificent mechanical entity alive with energy. Eric Lomax, in his memoir, The Railway Man, aptly noted, "The honest power of a steam engine is overwhelming."

As Big Boy took a half-hour break, spectators roamed about, capturing photographs from every angle while respecting the boundaries set by yellow tape. Despite the heat and noise, there was a sense of community among the crowd, united by shared excitement over Big Boy’s journey.

His journey concluded on July 4 in Philadelphia, coinciding with the country’s 250th anniversary celebration. Big Boy is now making the return trip, with a full itinerary available online. As he travels from California to the Eastern Seaboard and back, he symbolizes a time when railroads were vital in connecting our vast nation, reminding us of the hope that we remain united as "We, the People" today.

And perhaps, just perhaps, we can relearn the art of building trains.
